title: Stop Calling It a Rebrand When You Just Changed Your Logo
excerpt: A new logo isn't a rebrand. It's a design update. Real rebrands transform how your business operates, not just how it looks.
fbPost:
Another week, another "rebrand" announcement that's really just a new logo.

Here's the thing: a rebrand changes who you are in the market. It shifts your positioning, redefines your audience, transforms how you operate. A logo is just the symbol of your brand, not the brand itself.

When you call every design refresh a rebrand, you train your audience to ignore you. You waste the opportunity for real transformation. And you confuse cosmetic changes with strategic decisions.

Save "rebrand" for when you're actually changing something fundamental. Everything else is just a design update. Both are fine. Just know which one you're doing.

title: When a customer says they don't need it, they usually do
excerpt: Your customer isn't lying when they say they don't need what you sell. They're protecting a story they've been telling themselves for years.
fbPost:
Has a customer ever said "I don't need it" and you knew they absolutely did?

They're not lying. They're protecting a story. Maybe they got quoted too much once. Maybe they got burned. Maybe admitting they need it means admitting they should have bought it two years ago. Most of the time, "I don't need it" is just a defence mechanism with a really good cover story.

Your job isn't to argue them out of it. It's to make the next step small enough that the story doesn't have to defend itself. Low-risk. Specific. Obviously different from whatever burned them last time.

title: Industries Most Likely Impacted by AI in 2026
excerpt: Every generation fears the new tech. But this time the stakes are higher, the adoption curve steeper, and the jobs vanishing faster than you think.
fbPost:
Every generation fears the new thing. Cars, digital cameras, electric vehicles. The script is always the same: it won't work, it's not practical, my industry is too complex.

Then the new thing wins. Not because it's perfect. Because it's good enough and improving fast.

AI is following the same pattern, but faster. By 2026, customer service, content production, data entry, legal research, and commodity design will look completely different. The businesses that adapt early will win. The ones waiting for perfect clarity will lose ground they can't recover.

We spent 10,000 years evolving from industrious hunters to attention-seeking cat-picture enthusiasts. AI will probably follow the same curve, just condensed. Right now it's productive. Give it a few years and it might get lazy like us. Enjoy the gains while they last. 🚀

Read the full piece and figure out where you sit on the curve: [link]

#AI #SmallBusiness #Automation #BusinessStrategy #FutureOfWork
linkedinPost:
Every major technology shift gets dismissed the same way. Cars will never replace horses. Digital photos are pixelated rubbish. Electric vehicles need chargers everywhere.

The pattern is reliable: new tech arrives, incumbents resist, consumers cling to the familiar. Then the new thing wins.

AI is no different, except it's moving faster than anything before it. By 2026, these industries will see the most disruption:

Customer service. Chatbots already handle tier-one queries. In two years, they'll resolve 80% of tickets without escalation.

Content production. Copywriting, video editing, social scheduling. AI does this at speed and cost no human team can match.

Data entry and admin. Invoice processing, CRM updates, appointment scheduling. Keeping humans in these roles will be a luxury.

Legal research. Document review, contract analysis, compliance scanning. Junior-level work is already automatable.

Commodity design. Social templates, banner ads, basic layouts. High-end creative is safe. Resizing assets is not.

These aren't predictions. They're observations of what's happening at the leading edge. The only question is how fast everyone else catches up.

You can keep your head in the sand and insist your industry is special. Or you can accept where you sit on the adoption curve and act accordingly. Early adopters are already cutting costs and freeing teams for higher-value work. Laggards are still debating whether AI is real.

One odd thought to close: humans took 10,000 years to evolve from industrious hunters to attention-seeking vanity addicts. AI will probably follow the same trajectory, just faster. Right now it's productive. Give it a few years and it might discover TikTok and stop returning emails.

Adapt early. Fail fast. Keep moving. That's the model that survives.

Full article: [link]

#ArtificialIntelligence #BusinessStrategy #Automation #SmallBusiness #FutureOfWork

title: Can you really trust Ian?
excerpt: Ian wrote a piece predicting AI's future. Then he compared it to human laziness and TikTok addiction. Let's talk about whether you should take him seriously.
fbPost:
Ian just published a post predicting which industries AI will disrupt by 2026. Solid analysis. Then he compared AI development to human laziness and suggested AI might discover TikTok and ghost us all.

Which raises the question: can you trust a guy who goes from sober business advice to "AI will get lazy like we did"?

Short answer: yes, but with context. The first half of his post is spot on. Customer service, content production, data entry, legal research, commodity design. These are already being automated. If your business relies on repetitive tasks, you're in the blast radius.

The second half? That's Ian being Ian. Fun theory, not grounded in research, but entertaining. The useful bit: AI inherits our flaws. It's not perfect. It's good enough and improving fast.

So take what's useful. Ignore the rest. And if you need help figuring out where AI fits in your business without the speculative detours, hit the link. 👇

#SmallBusiness #AIAdoption #Automation #BusinessStrategy #PlainBlack
linkedinPost:
Ian just published a piece on which industries AI will disrupt by 2026. The first half is sharp and specific. The second half compares AI development to human laziness and suggests AI might end up on TikTok.

So, can you trust him?

Yes. With context.

The industries Ian lists are already being automated. Customer service, content production, data entry, legal research, commodity design work. This is not speculation. It's observation. The early adopters are cutting costs and speeding up operations. The laggards are still debating whether AI is real.

Then Ian pivots to a theory about AI inheriting human laziness. It's entertaining. It's not grounded in research. But the core point holds: AI is built by humans. It inherits our biases, our shortcuts, our flaws. It's not perfect. It's good enough and improving fast.

The takeaway for small business owners: don't wait for perfect clarity. Ask where in your business you're still doing repetitive tasks that could be automated. Automate the repetitive. Delegate the commodity. Keep the humans for strategy, relationships, and creative problem solving.

That's the model that survives. Whether or not AI ends up scrolling cat videos.

Full piece on the PlainBlack blog. Link in comments.

#SmallBusiness #AIStrategy #Automation #BusinessGrowth #PlainBlack

linkedinPost:
We're building 30 products in 30 days starting 1 May 2026.

One product built and published every single day. Jay builds it. Ian makes a daily video about what was built and why it works. All in public. All tracked.

But here's the real challenge.

Both of us are ADHD. We are genuinely terrible at posting consistently. We have great ideas and then we don't execute them. Our wives have literally said: "You have great ideas, but then you have to fucking do them. You suck at that."

So the real question is: can two ADHD founders stay focused, stay consistent, and follow through on something for 30 days straight?

We built the challenge dashboard specifically to solve our own problem. It gives us a plan to follow so there are no decisions to make each day. It tracks wins and results so we get the endorphin hit that keeps ADHD brains interested. It logs the analytics so we can prove to ourselves that this isn't just another abandoned 30-day challenge.

The problem we're solving for ourselves is exactly the problem our ICP has. Small business owners who start things and don't finish them. Who have a crack at posting and give up after two weeks. Who know they should be doing this stuff but can't stay consistent.

We are the target customer. We built the tool for ourselves first.

What we're building: lead generation tools, filtering tools that stop tyre-kickers, conversion tools, clarity tools that help customers decide faster. All built for real small business niches. Tradies, cafes, professional services, retail.

We're tracking everything. Weekly reports with real numbers: reach, DMs, enquiries, revenue. And the human stuff too: is it still fun? Are we still enthusiastic? Is it actually working?

AI has closed the gap between a good idea and a finished deliverable. We have the ideas, the taste, and now the tools to ship fast. This challenge is 30 days of proof.

Follow along. Watch the weekly reports. Spot one that fits your business? We'll wire it up for you.

#SmallBusiness #BuildInPublic #ADHD #AITools #PlainBlack

title: Today I built: a 404 page that doesn't tell customers to check their spelling
excerpt: A 404 page that emails me when real broken links get hit, searches for what visitors actually typed, and turns dead ends into a drag-and-drop game.
fbPost:
Most 404 pages hand you a sad face and watch you bounce. The business never knows you tried.

Today I rebuilt PlainBlack's 404 so it does five jobs instead: emails me when a real broken link gets hit, pre-fills a search box with what you actually typed, runs a drag-and-drop game with a live leaderboard, and turns the whole dead end into a brand moment. All on free-tier infrastructure.

The alternative? Wait a week for a customer to mention it. Email your dev. They say "I'll take a look." Invoice arrives for $120. Every visitor who hit that same link in the meantime bounced straight to your competitor.

Your 404 is either a leak or a recovery tool. Try the one we built: plainblackcreative.com/404

#WebDesign #SmallBusiness #CustomerExperience #BrandStrategy #PlainBlack
linkedinPost:
Most small businesses find broken links when a customer eventually mentions it. Then they email the dev. A week passes. Invoice arrives.

In that week, every other visitor who hit the same dead link bounced. Straight back to Google. Straight to a competitor. You'll never know how many.

Today I rebuilt PlainBlack's 404 page to collapse that whole cycle. It does five jobs:

- Emails me the moment a real broken link is hit (filters out internal visits so I don't drown in noise)
- Pre-fills a search box with the words from the URL the visitor actually tried
- Runs a drag-and-drop game with a live leaderboard that brings repeat visitors back
- Offers three contextual route cards for visitors who don't want to play
- Turns the error into a brand moment, not a brand crack

The search decision took the longest. My first instinct was to build an internal index — keep the visitor on PlainBlack the whole time, branded results, no detour. Then I compared it line by line to Google's site: operator. Google wins on 9 criteria, internal wins on 2, three are a tie. The "leaving the site" framing makes it sound like a leak. In practice it's a 3-second detour through a Google-powered, ad-free, competitor-free results page that shows nothing but my work. Free Search Console signals every time a visitor searches.

All on free-tier infrastructure. No monthly subscription. No usage caps to babysit.

Your 404 is either a leak in the bottom of your funnel or a recovery tool. Try the one we built: plainblackcreative.com/404

#WebDesign #SmallBusiness #CustomerExperience #BrandStrategy #PlainBlack

title: Social Sharing That Actually Works: OG Images, Specs & Tools
excerpt: Every platform has different rules for link previews. Get the specs, tools, and image prompts you need to make your social shares look professional.
fbPost:
You paste a link into LinkedIn and it looks fine. Same link on Facebook? Cropped, blurry, or missing entirely.

Every platform has different rules for link previews. Different image sizes, file types, behaviours. If you want your shares to look professional, you need the right specs and a process.

New post breaks down OG images, platform specs (spoiler: LinkedIn hates WebP), and the exact tools you need: Facebook Debugger, LinkedIn Post Inspector, Squoosh for resizing, ChatGPT for image gen. Plus prompt tips and why alt text matters.

Read it here: [link]

#SocialMedia #Marketing #OGImages #SmallBusiness #DigitalMarketing
linkedinPost:
You update your website, share the link on LinkedIn, and the preview image is a random screenshot from three years ago.

Platforms cache OG images aggressively. If you don't set them correctly and test before you share, you lose control of how your content appears.

Every platform has different specs. Facebook wants 1200×630px. LinkedIn doesn't support WebP. Instagram and TikTok don't pull previews at all because they don't allow clickable links in posts.

The fix is a five-minute workflow: create the image (ChatGPT or Midjourney work), resize it in Squoosh, set your og:image and og:image:alt tags, then test in the Facebook Sharing Debugger and LinkedIn Post Inspector before you go live.

I wrote a full breakdown of platform specs, debug tools, image generation prompts, and alt text best practice. If you're sharing links on social and want them to look professional, this is the checklist.

Read it here: [link]

#SocialMedia #OGImages #Marketing #SmallBusiness #DigitalMarketing

title: More roofing leads are not better if half of them waste your quoting time
excerpt: Most roofing websites are built to collect every lead. This one is built to protect quoting time.
fbPost:
More leads are not always better. Especially if you're paying for them.

For roofers, a bad-fit enquiry can waste ad spend, admin time, follow-ups, a site visit, and an evening spent pricing a job that was never serious. So we built a demo: The Roof Quote Fit Checker. It shows how a roofing business could filter enquiries before they hit the inbox. Good-fit leads get the quote CTA. Maybe-fit leads get asked for more detail first. Bad-fit leads do not get sent straight to the contact form.

Most websites are built to collect every lead. This one is built to protect quoting time. Run the maths on a roofer fielding 10 quote requests a week, 4 of them poor-fit, 45 minutes each to check, price, reply, follow up. That's 3 hours wasted per week. 144 hours per year. Before you count the ad spend.

See the demo and strategic walkthrough on the PlainBlack blog. Link in bio. 🛠️

#RoofingBusiness #LeadQuality #TradeMarketing #SmallBusinessNZ #PlainBlack
linkedinPost:
More leads are not always better. Especially if you're paying for them.

For roofers, a bad-fit enquiry does not just waste inbox space. It can waste ad spend, admin time, follow-ups, a site visit, and an evening spent pricing a job that was never serious.

So we built a demo: The Roof Quote Fit Checker. It asks six questions a roofer would normally ask on the first phone call, then routes the enquiry into one of three buckets. Good-fit leads get the quote CTA. Maybe-fit leads get asked for more detail first. Bad-fit leads do not get sent straight to the contact form.

The strategic move is not making the form longer. It's stopping the wrong people from reaching the same finish line as the right ones.

Run the maths on a roofer fielding 10 quote requests a week, 4 of them poor-fit, 45 minutes each to check, price, reply, follow up. That's 3 hours wasted per week. 12 hours per month. 144 hours per year. Before you count the ad spend that brought those bad-fit leads in.

If a lead costs money to get, every bad-fit quote request hits twice. First in ad spend. Then in time. That's a shit deal.

Most websites are built to collect every lead. This one is built to protect quoting time. Same logic works for plumbers, sparkies, HVAC, builders, landscapers, decks and fences, anyone running quote-heavy trade work where the inbox is starting to outpace the site visits.

See the demo and strategic walkthrough on the PlainBlack blog.

#RoofingBusiness #LeadQuality #TradeMarketing #SmallBusinessStrategy #PlainBlack

title: We built the tracker before we built anything else
excerpt: Day 4 of 30. We published the public build tracker before we shipped any of the customer-facing builds. Here's why, and what's on it.
fbPost:
Day 4 of 30. We shipped the public build tracker today.

It lives at plainblackcreative.com/challenge. Live numbers, real cards for every shipped day, 'Coming soon' stubs for the days we haven't done yet. The page IS the accountability mechanism.

Three days in, here is what it shows. Day 1: 176 reach. Day 2: 358 reach. Day 3: 1 reach.

Yeah, 1. The roofer-quote-checker post landed Saturday afternoon and basically nobody was on Facebook to see it. We could have buried that number. We didn't. The page shows it.

Why this comes before any of the customer builds: anyone running a 30-day challenge can quietly skip a day and pretend it never happened. Now we can't.

If we don't ship Day 5 tomorrow, you will see a 'Coming soon' card sitting there. Send Ian a passive-aggressive comment. We'll know we deserved it.

Read the post + watch the live tracker: link in bio.

#BuildInPublic #SmallBusiness #PlainBlack #30DayChallenge

title: Coming to us for a "website" is like going to a restaurant for "food"
excerpt: Day 5 of 30. I stopped building a demo for someone else and started building the tool we actually needed: a menu that lets you order the business asset you're hungry for, not just "a website."
fbPost:
You don't go to a restaurant and order "food." So why do we tell people to come to us for "a website"?

Day 5 of the 30-day challenge. I started building a demo for someone else and ended up building the tool we actually needed. It's called the Build Map. You tap the problems you have, it shows you the business asset that solves them, and you configure the scope with a live budget gauge that breathes red to amber to mint as your build changes shape.

It's a menu. A working menu, sorted by purpose. You order what you're actually hungry for. Front Door, Clear Offer, Lead Engine, Business System. 22 components. Three DIY swap states. Hybrid builds when your problems span two shapes. All live, all unfinished, all visible seams.

Try it and tell me what feels off: https://plainblackcreative.com/build-map

#BuildInPublic #30DayChallenge #SmallBusiness #WebDesign #ServiceBusiness
linkedinPost:
Coming to us for a "website" is like going to a restaurant for "food."

Day 5 of 30. I stopped building a demo for someone else and started building the tool we actually needed.

Most service businesses present their offer as a list. "We do branding. We do websites. We do marketing." Customers stare at it like a menu in a language they don't speak. The Build Map flips it. The customer doesn't pick a service. They pick a problem. The tool maps the problem to the services that actually solve it.

You tap the problems you have. The tool picks one of four build shapes: Front Door, Clear Offer, Lead Engine, or Business System. It shows you the 22 components that make it real. Done-for-you, guided DIY, or skip. Set a budget band, and the bar breathes red to amber to mint as the total moves with what you've configured. Hybrid leans show up when your problems span two shapes. Weak-point questions pop in when you need them. Do you already own your domain? Do you have your logo as a vector file? Yes saves you a bit. No keeps the price honest.

This isn't a marketing trick. It's how we already think when a real client emails us. We don't sell them "a website." We figure out what they're trying to do and recommend the build shape that gets them there. The Build Map is just that conversation, rendered as a configurable thing on a public URL.

It's unfinished. Pricing is placeholder. The comparison cards aren't built yet. The brand pathway isn't built yet. I ran out of day before I ran out of ideas. But it's live, and the whole point of building in public is that you get to see the seams.

Try it: https://plainblackcreative.com/build-map

#BuildInPublic #30DayChallenge #SmallBusiness #ProfessionalServices #ProductDesign
